description: A fundamental conjunction used to connect two contrasting clauses or ideas.

exampleSentences.german: Ich bin müde, aber ich muss arbeiten.
exampleSentences.english: I am tired, but I have to work.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं थका हुआ हूँ, लेकिन मुझे काम करना है।

exampleSentences.german: Das Auto ist alt, aber schnell.
exampleSentences.english: The car is old but fast.
exampleSentences.hindi: कार पुरानी है, लेकिन तेज़ है।

usageContext: Used daily in almost any conversation to introduce a contradiction or exception.
grammarNotes: It is a coordinating conjunction (position 0), meaning it does not affect the word order of the following clause.

description: The cardinal number following seven and preceding nine.

exampleSentences.english: I bought eight apples.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैंने आठ सेब खरीदे।

exampleSentences.english: It is eight o'clock in the morning.
exampleSentences.hindi: सुबह के आठ बजे हैं।

usageContext: Used for counting, time, ages, and quantities.
grammarNotes: As a number, it remains indeclinable in most basic contexts.

description: Used to refer to the whole quantity or every member of a group; can also colloquially mean something is finished/gone.

exampleSentences.english: All children are playing in the garden.
exampleSentences.hindi: सभी बच्चे बगीचे में खेल रहे हैं।

exampleSentences.english: The milk is all gone.
exampleSentences.hindi: दूध खत्म हो गया है।

usageContext: Used for people (everyone) or as a determiner for plural nouns. Colloquially used to mean 'finished'.
grammarNotes: When used as a determiner, it follows the declension of the definite article.

description: An indefinite pronoun referring to every thing or the whole situation.

exampleSentences.german: Alles ist bereit für die Party.
exampleSentences.english: Everything is ready for the party.
exampleSentences.hindi: पार्टी के लिए सब कुछ तैयार है।

exampleSentences.english: Is everything okay with you?
exampleSentences.hindi: क्या तुम्हारे साथ सब ठीक है?

usageContext: Used to summarize a situation or refer to a collective group of objects.
grammarNotes: Used in the singular neuter form to encompass a whole concept or group of things.

description: A versatile word used for past events (when), comparisons (than), or roles (as).

exampleSentences.german: Er ist größer als sein Bruder.
exampleSentences.english: He is taller than his brother.
exampleSentences.hindi: वह अपने भाई से लंबा है।

exampleSentences.german: Als ich ein Kind war, wohnte ich in Berlin.
exampleSentences.english: When I was a child, I lived in Berlin.
exampleSentences.hindi: जब मैं बच्चा था, मैं बर्लिन में रहता था।

usageContext: Crucial for comparisons and for specific singular events in the past.
grammarNotes: When used as 'when', it triggers subclause word order (verb at the end) and refers to the past.

description: A two-way preposition used to describe location (on top of something) or direction (onto something).

exampleSentences.german: Das Buch liegt auf dem Tisch.
exampleSentences.english: The book is lying on the table.
exampleSentences.hindi: किताब मेज पर रखी है।

exampleSentences.english: I am waiting for the bus.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं बस का इंतज़ार कर रहा हूँ।

usageContext: Commonly used for horizontal surfaces or in fixed phrases like 'waiting for' (warten auf).
grammarNotes: It is a Wechselpräposition; it takes the dative for location (where?) and accusative for movement (where to?).

description: Used to indicate origin (where someone is from) or moving out from inside a space.

exampleSentences.english: I come from India.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं भारत से हूँ।

exampleSentences.english: Go out of the house!
exampleSentences.hindi: घर से बाहर जाओ!

usageContext: Essential for stating nationality/origin and describing exiting an enclosed space.
grammarNotes: Always requires the dative case.

description: Used to describe being at a person's home, a company, or a specific location/nearness.

exampleSentences.english: I am at my friend's house.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं अपने दोस्त के यहाँ हूँ।

exampleSentences.english: He works at Google.
exampleSentences.hindi: वह गूगल में काम करता है।

usageContext: Used when staying with someone or working for a specific company name.
grammarNotes: Always requires the dative case. Often contracted with 'dem' to form 'beim'.

description: Indicates a limit in time or space.

exampleSentences.english: Until tomorrow!
exampleSentences.hindi: कल तक!

exampleSentences.english: The train goes as far as Berlin.
exampleSentences.hindi: ट्रेन बर्लिन तक जाती है।

usageContext: Common in farewells (Bis bald, Bis dann) and for setting deadlines.
grammarNotes: Usually takes the accusative case, though often used with other prepositions (bis zu).

description: A multi-purpose politeness marker used for requests, responses to 'thanks', or asking for clarification.

exampleSentences.english: A coffee, please.
exampleSentences.hindi: एक कॉफ़ी, कृपया।

exampleSentences.english: Pardon? / What did you say?
exampleSentences.hindi: क्षमा करें, क्या कहा?

usageContext: Essential for polite daily interaction. Used as a reply to 'Danke'.
grammarNotes: Derived from the verb 'bitten' (to ask/request).

description: A polite response to 'Danke' or a phrase used when handing something to someone.

exampleSentences.english: Here you go, here is your change.
exampleSentences.hindi: यह लीजिए, ये रहे आपके खुले पैसे।

exampleSentences.english: Thanks for the help! - You're welcome!
exampleSentences.hindi: मदद के लिए धन्यवाद! - आपका स्वागत है!

usageContext: Used commonly in service industries (waiters, shopkeepers) or as a polite reply to a thank you.
grammarNotes: An intensified form of 'bitte'. Can be used as a question 'Bitteschön?' to mean 'Yes, how can I help you?'.

description: The standard way to express gratitude in German.

exampleSentences.english: Thank you for the gift!
exampleSentences.hindi: तोहफे के लिए धन्यवाद!

exampleSentences.english: Would you like water? - No, thank you.
exampleSentences.hindi: क्या आप पानी लेना चाहेंगे? - नहीं, शुक्रिया।

usageContext: Used in any situation where gratitude is expressed. Note: Saying 'Danke' when offered something often implies 'No, thank you'.
grammarNotes: Derived from the verb 'danken'. In formal writing, 'Vielen Dank' is often preferred.

description: A subordinating conjunction used to introduce a dependent clause.

exampleSentences.german: Ich weiß, dass du recht hast.
exampleSentences.english: I know that you are right.
exampleSentences.hindi: मुझे पता है कि तुम सही हो।

exampleSentences.german: Es ist schön, dass du da bist.
exampleSentences.english: It is nice that you are here.
exampleSentences.hindi: यह अच्छा है कि तुम यहाँ हो।

usageContext: Used to link a main clause with a statement or fact in a subclause.
grammarNotes: Always sends the conjugated verb to the very end of the sentence. Not to be confused with the relative pronoun 'das'.

description: The informal possessive pronoun for 'du' (you).

exampleSentences.english: Is that your book?
exampleSentences.hindi: क्या यह तुम्हारी किताब है?

exampleSentences.english: What is your name?
exampleSentences.hindi: तुम्हारा नाम क्या है?

usageContext: Used when speaking to friends, family, children, or people you are on familiar terms with.
grammarNotes: It must be declined according to the gender, case, and number of the noun it precedes (e.g., deine Mutter, deinem Vater).

description: A coordinating conjunction used to give a reason, or a modal particle used to soften questions.

exampleSentences.german: Ich bleibe zu Hause, denn ich bin krank.
exampleSentences.english: I am staying at home, because I am sick.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं घर पर रह रहा हूँ क्योंकि मैं बीमार हूँ।

exampleSentences.english: What time is it then? / Tell me, what time is it?
exampleSentences.hindi: तो फिर समय क्या हुआ है?

usageContext: Used to connect two main clauses. As a particle, it makes questions sound more curious and less like an interrogation.
grammarNotes: As a conjunction, it occupies 'position 0' and does not change the word order. Unlike 'weil', which sends the verb to the end.

description: A demonstrative pronoun or determiner used to point out a specific person or thing nearby.

exampleSentences.english: This wine tastes very good.
exampleSentences.hindi: यह वाइन बहुत अच्छी है।

exampleSentences.english: This man is my teacher.
exampleSentences.hindi: यह आदमी मेरे शिक्षक हैं।

usageContext: Used when identifying a specific object or person in sight or previously mentioned.
grammarNotes: The ending changes based on gender and case (dieser, diese, dieses, diesen, etc.).

description: The cardinal number between two and four.

exampleSentences.english: I have three siblings.
exampleSentences.hindi: मेरे तीन भाई-बहन हैं।

exampleSentences.english: That costs three euros.
exampleSentences.hindi: इसकी कीमत तीन यूरो है।

usageContext: Standard counting and expressing quantities.
grammarNotes: Generally indeclinable unless used in rare archaic genitive forms.

description: The cardinal number representing 3 tens.

exampleSentences.english: He is thirty years old.
exampleSentences.hindi: वह तीस साल का है।

exampleSentences.english: It is thirty degrees outside.
exampleSentences.hindi: बाहर तीस डिग्री तापमान है।

usageContext: Used for age, temperature, and large quantities.
grammarNotes: Note the use of 'ß' in 'dreißig', unlike other tens like 'vierzig' or 'fünfzig' which use 'z'.

description: The informal second-person singular personal pronoun.

exampleSentences.english: What is your name?
exampleSentences.hindi: तुम्हारा नाम क्या है?

exampleSentences.english: Do you come from Germany?
exampleSentences.hindi: क्या तुम जर्मनी से हो?

usageContext: Used with friends, family, children, and peers.
grammarNotes: Requires verbs to end in '-st' in the present tense (e.g., du machst).

description: Indicates movement from one side to another or the method/agent used to achieve something.

exampleSentences.english: We are walking through the park.
exampleSentences.hindi: हम पार्क के बीच में से जा रहे हैं।

exampleSentences.german: Ich habe es durch das Internet gelernt.
exampleSentences.english: I learned it through the internet.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैंने इसे इंटरनेट के माध्यम से सीखा।

usageContext: Used for physical passage or for defining an instrument/reason for an action.
grammarNotes: Always takes the accusative case.

description: An indefinite pronoun used to indicate an unspecified number of things or people.

exampleSentences.english: I have some questions.
exampleSentences.hindi: मेरे पास कुछ सवाल हैं।

exampleSentences.english: Several people have already left.
exampleSentences.hindi: कई लोग पहले ही जा चुके हैं।

usageContext: Used when referring to more than one but not a large amount.
grammarNotes: It functions as an adjective/pronoun and takes plural endings (einige, einiger, einigen).

description: The cardinal number 'one' used when counting or as a pronoun.

exampleSentences.english: One, two, three, go!
exampleSentences.hindi: एक, दो, तीन, शुरू!

exampleSentences.english: I only have one (thing).
exampleSentences.hindi: मेरे पास केवल एक है।

usageContext: Used for counting (abstractly) or as a replacement for a neuter noun (das Ding -> eins).
grammarNotes: When followed by a noun, it changes to 'ein/eine/ein'. 'Eins' is used primarily for counting or standing alone.

description: The standard polite term for getting someone's attention or apologizing for a minor mistake.

exampleSentences.english: Excuse me, where is the train station?
exampleSentences.hindi: माफ़ कीजिए, रेलवे स्टेशन कहाँ है?

exampleSentences.english: Sorry for the delay.
exampleSentences.hindi: देरी के लिए माफ़ी चाहता हूँ।

usageContext: Used both to interrupt someone politely and to apologize.
grammarNotes: As a noun, it is feminine (die Entschuldigung). As an exclamation, the 'E' is capitalized.

description: Third-person singular masculine personal pronoun.

exampleSentences.english: He comes from Spain.
exampleSentences.hindi: वह स्पेन से है।

exampleSentences.german: Der Tisch ist alt. Er ist kaputt.
exampleSentences.english: The table is old. It (he) is broken.
exampleSentences.hindi: मेज पुरानी है। वह टूटी हुई है।

usageContext: Refers to a male person or any masculine noun (der-word).
grammarNotes: In the nominative case. Changes to 'ihn' (accusative) and 'ihm' (dative).

description: Third-person singular neuter personal pronoun.

exampleSentences.english: How is it going with you?
exampleSentences.hindi: तुम कैसे हो?

exampleSentences.german: Das ist ein Kind. Es spielt.
exampleSentences.english: That is a child. It is playing.
exampleSentences.hindi: वह एक बच्चा है। वह खेल रहा है।

usageContext: Refers to neuter nouns (das-words) or used as a placeholder (e.g., 'Es regnet' - It's raining).
grammarNotes: Remains 'es' in both nominative and accusative cases.

description: An indefinite pronoun used to refer to an unspecified object, situation, or a small amount of something.

exampleSentences.english: Would you like something to drink?
exampleSentences.hindi: क्या आप कुछ पीना चाहेंगे?

exampleSentences.english: That is a bit expensive.
exampleSentences.hindi: यह थोड़ा महँगा है।

usageContext: Commonly used in offers, requests, or to soften an adjective (like 'a bit'). In casual speech, it is often shortened to 'was'.
grammarNotes: It is indeclinable, meaning it never changes its form regardless of the case.

description: The informal possessive pronoun for a group of people (plural 'you' / ihr).

exampleSentences.english: Is that your house?
exampleSentences.hindi: क्या यह तुम्हारा घर है?

exampleSentences.english: Where are your children?
exampleSentences.hindi: तुम्हारे बच्चे कहाँ हैं?

usageContext: Used when addressing two or more people with whom you are on 'du' terms (friends, family).
grammarNotes: It declines like 'mein'. Note: The 'e' before 'r' is often dropped when an ending is added (e.g., eure, eurer, eurem).

description: The cardinal number between four and six.

exampleSentences.english: It is five o'clock.
exampleSentences.hindi: पाँच बजे हैं।

exampleSentences.german: Ich habe fünf Finger an einer Hand.
exampleSentences.english: I have five fingers on one hand.
exampleSentences.hindi: मेरे एक हाथ में पाँच उंगलियाँ हैं।

usageContext: Standard counting for time, money, and quantities.
grammarNotes: The pronunciation of the 'ü' (umlaut) is key here; it sounds like a tight 'ee' with rounded lips.

description: A preposition used to indicate a recipient, a purpose, or a duration of time.

exampleSentences.english: This gift is for you.
exampleSentences.hindi: यह तोहफा तुम्हारे लिए है।

exampleSentences.english: I am studying for the exam.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं परीक्षा के लिए पढ़ रहा हूँ।

usageContext: Essential for expressing benefit, exchange, or intent.
grammarNotes: Always requires the accusative case.

description: Used to show opposition, physical contact against something, or an approximate time.

exampleSentences.german: Wir spielen gegen eine starke Mannschaft.
exampleSentences.english: We are playing against a strong team.
exampleSentences.hindi: हम एक मजबूत टीम के खिलाफ खेल रहे हैं।

exampleSentences.english: I'll come around eight o'clock.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं लगभग आठ बजे आऊँगा।

usageContext: Used for sports rivalries, physical leaning, or stating an approximate time of arrival.
grammarNotes: Always requires the accusative case.

description: An adverb used to give directions, indicating movement in a straight line without turning.

exampleSentences.german: Gehen Sie immer geradeaus bis zur Ampel.
exampleSentences.english: Go straight ahead until the traffic light.
exampleSentences.hindi: ट्रैफिक लाइट तक सीधे चलते रहें।

exampleSentences.german: Fahren Sie an der Kreuzung geradeaus.
exampleSentences.english: Go straight at the intersection.
exampleSentences.hindi: चौराहे से सीधे आगे बढ़ें।

usageContext: Essential for giving or following travel directions on foot or by car.
grammarNotes: It is an adverb and does not decline.

description: The most common and versatile informal greeting in German.

exampleSentences.english: Hello, how are you?
exampleSentences.hindi: नमस्ते, तुम कैसे हो?

exampleSentences.english: Hello everyone!
exampleSentences.hindi: सबको नमस्ते!

usageContext: Used with friends, family, and colleagues. In very formal business settings, 'Guten Tag' is preferred.
grammarNotes: It can be used at any time of the day.

description: A two-way preposition indicating a position at the rear of something.

exampleSentences.german: Das Auto parkt hinter dem Haus.
exampleSentences.english: The car is parked behind the house.
exampleSentences.hindi: कार घर के पीछे खड़ी है।

exampleSentences.english: Stand behind me.
exampleSentences.hindi: मेरे पीछे खड़े हो जाओ।

usageContext: Used to describe physical location or relative position.
grammarNotes: A 'Wechselpräposition': uses Dative for location (where?) and Accusative for movement (where to?).

description: The cardinal number 100.

exampleSentences.english: The ticket costs a hundred euros.
exampleSentences.hindi: टिकट की कीमत सौ यूरो है।

exampleSentences.german: Es waren fast hundert Leute da.
exampleSentences.english: There were almost a hundred people there.
exampleSentences.hindi: वहाँ लगभग सौ लोग थे।

usageContext: Used for prices, quantities, and centuries.
grammarNotes: Can be used as 'hundert' or 'einhundert' interchangeably.

description: The first-person singular personal pronoun.

exampleSentences.english: My name is Rahul (I am called Rahul).
exampleSentences.hindi: मेरा नाम राहुल है।

exampleSentences.english: I am learning German.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं जर्मन सीख रहा हूँ।

usageContext: The subject used to refer to oneself.
grammarNotes: In the Nominative case. Changes to 'mich' (Accusative) and 'mir' (Dative). Verbs usually end in '-e'.

description: A very versatile word that can act as a personal pronoun (you all) or a possessive pronoun (her/their/your).

exampleSentences.english: You (all) are very friendly.
exampleSentences.hindi: तुम लोग बहुत दयालु हो।

exampleSentences.english: That is her book (or their book).
exampleSentences.hindi: यह उसकी किताब है।

usageContext: Used to address a group informally, or to indicate possession for a female or a group.
grammarNotes: As a personal pronoun, it takes specific verb endings (-t). As a possessive, it must decline based on the noun it follows.

description: A two-way preposition indicating location inside a space or movement into a space.

exampleSentences.english: I am in the kitchen.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं रसोई में हूँ।

exampleSentences.english: We are going into the cinema.
exampleSentences.hindi: हम सिनेमा हॉल जा रहे हैं।

usageContext: Used for physical locations, cities, and countries (with articles), or time periods (in a week).
grammarNotes: Takes the dative case for static location (Wo?) and accusative case for movement (Wohin?).

description: Used to refer to every single person or thing in a group.

exampleSentences.english: Everyone needs love.
exampleSentences.hindi: हर किसी को प्यार की ज़रूरत होती है।

exampleSentences.english: I go for a walk every day.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं हर दिन सैर पर जाता हूँ।

usageContext: Used to generalize or emphasize that no one/nothing is excluded.
grammarNotes: Declines like the definite article 'der' (jeder, jede, jedes).

description: Refers to an unspecified person.

exampleSentences.english: Is someone there?
exampleSentences.hindi: क्या कोई वहाँ है?

exampleSentences.english: Someone called me.
exampleSentences.hindi: किसी ने मुझे फोन किया।

usageContext: Used when the identity of the person is unknown or irrelevant.
grammarNotes: Can be used with or without case endings in the dative/accusative (jemandem/jemanden).

description: A demonstrative pronoun used to point out a person or thing further away, or to contrast with 'dieser' (this).

exampleSentences.german: An jenem Tag war es sehr kalt.
exampleSentences.english: On that (particular) day, it was very cold.
exampleSentences.hindi: उस दिन बहुत ठंड थी।

exampleSentences.german: Dieser Film ist gut, aber jener ist besser.
exampleSentences.english: This movie is good, but that one is better.
exampleSentences.hindi: यह फिल्म अच्छी है, लेकिन वह वाली बेहतर है।

usageContext: Less common in spoken German than 'der/die/das da', but frequent in literature and formal writing.
grammarNotes: Declines exactly like 'dieser' (jener, jene, jenes).

description: The first-person singular possessive pronoun used to indicate ownership or relationship by the speaker.

exampleSentences.english: That is my car.
exampleSentences.hindi: यह मेरी कार है।

exampleSentences.english: My brother lives in Berlin.
exampleSentences.hindi: मेरा भाई बर्लिन में रहता है।

usageContext: Used constantly in daily life to describe personal belongings, family members, or personal attributes.
grammarNotes: Follows 'ein-word' declension; endings change based on the gender and case of the noun that follows (e.g., meine Mutter, meinem Vater).

description: A preposition used to indicate company, instrument, or means of transport.

exampleSentences.german: Ich gehe mit meiner Freundin ins Kino.
exampleSentences.english: I am going to the cinema with my girlfriend.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं अपनी सहेली के साथ सिनेमा जा रहा हूँ।

exampleSentences.english: I am traveling by train.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं ट्रेन से जा रहा हूँ।

usageContext: Used when doing something together with someone or using a tool/vehicle.
grammarNotes: Always requires the dative case.

description: A preposition used for directions toward cities/countries, time (after), or citing a source.

exampleSentences.german: Nach dem Essen trinke ich einen Tee.
exampleSentences.english: After eating, I drink a tea.
exampleSentences.hindi: खाने के बाद मैं चाय पीता हूँ।

exampleSentences.english: I am flying to Germany.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं जर्मनी जा रहा हूँ।

usageContext: Used for travel to specific geographical names (cities, countries without articles) and for telling time (minutes 'after' the hour).
grammarNotes: Always requires the dative case.

description: A two-way preposition indicating physical proximity or side-by-side position.

exampleSentences.german: Die Post ist neben der Bank.
exampleSentences.english: The post office is next to the bank.
exampleSentences.hindi: डाकघर बैंक के बगल में है।

exampleSentences.english: Sit down next to me.
exampleSentences.hindi: मेरे बगल में बैठ जाओ।

usageContext: Used to describe the location of objects or people in relation to each other.
grammarNotes: A 'Wechselpräposition': takes the dative for location (Wo?) and accusative for movement (Wohin?).

description: The cardinal number between eight and ten.

exampleSentences.english: It is nine o'clock in the evening.
exampleSentences.hindi: शाम के नौ बजे हैं।

exampleSentences.english: I have nine euros left.
exampleSentences.hindi: मेरे पास नौ यूरो बचे हैं।

usageContext: Standard counting, time, and quantities.
grammarNotes: The 'eu' diphthong is pronounced like 'oy' in English.

description: An indefinite pronoun used to indicate the absence of any thing or object.

exampleSentences.english: I have nothing to eat.
exampleSentences.hindi: मेरे पास खाने के लिए कुछ नहीं है।

exampleSentences.english: That doesn't matter (literally: That makes nothing).
exampleSentences.hindi: कोई बात नहीं।

usageContext: Used to negate the existence of objects or ideas. 'Nichts' is never capitalized unless it's at the start of a sentence or used as a noun (das Nichts).
grammarNotes: It is indeclinable and remains 'nichts' in all cases.

description: An indefinite pronoun used to refer to the absence of any person.

exampleSentences.english: Nobody is at home.
exampleSentences.hindi: घर पर कोई नहीं है।

exampleSentences.english: I told nobody about it.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैंने इसके बारे में किसी को नहीं बताया।

usageContext: Used when referring to people specifically. In casual speech, 'keiner' is often used instead.
grammarNotes: Can be used with or without case endings (niemandem for dative, niemanden for accusative), though endings are more common in formal writing.

description: A subordinating conjunction used to introduce indirect questions or doubts.

exampleSentences.german: Ich weiß nicht, ob er kommt.
exampleSentences.english: I don't know whether he is coming.
exampleSentences.hindi: मुझे नहीं पता कि वह आएगा या नहीं।

exampleSentences.german: Frag ihn, ob er Zeit hat.
exampleSentences.english: Ask him if he has time.
exampleSentences.hindi: उससे पूछो कि क्या उसके पास समय है।

usageContext: Used for 'yes/no' type indirect questions. Not to be confused with 'wenn' (if/when).
grammarNotes: As a subordinating conjunction, it sends the conjugated verb to the very end of the clause.

description: A coordinating conjunction used to connect two or more alternatives.

exampleSentences.english: Would you like tea or coffee?
exampleSentences.hindi: क्या आप चाय लेंगे या कॉफ़ी?

exampleSentences.english: We are going today or tomorrow.
exampleSentences.hindi: हम आज या कल जाएंगे।

usageContext: Commonly used in questions or to offer choices. Often added to the end of a sentence as a tag question like '...right?'.
grammarNotes: Occupies 'position 0' in a sentence; it does not change the word order of the following clause.

description: A preposition indicating the absence or exclusion of someone or something.

exampleSentences.english: I drink coffee without sugar.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं बिना चीनी के कॉफ़ी पीता हूँ।

exampleSentences.english: I won't go without you.
exampleSentences.hindi: तुम्हारे बिना मैं नहीं जाऊँगा।

usageContext: Used for both physical items and people.
grammarNotes: Always requires the accusative case.

description: The cardinal number between five and seven.

exampleSentences.english: We are six people.
exampleSentences.hindi: हम छह लोग हैं।

exampleSentences.german: Das Kind ist sechs Jahre alt.
exampleSentences.english: The child is six years old.
exampleSentences.hindi: बच्चा छह साल का है।

usageContext: Standard counting for time, quantity, or age.
grammarNotes: The 's' at the beginning is voiced (like a 'z'), and 'chs' is pronounced like 'ks'.

description: A temporal preposition used to describe an action that started in the past and is still continuing.

exampleSentences.german: Ich wohne seit zwei Jahren in Berlin.
exampleSentences.english: I have been living in Berlin for two years.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं दो साल से बर्लिन में रह रहा हूँ।

exampleSentences.english: Since when have you been learning German?
exampleSentences.hindi: तुम कब से जर्मन सीख रहे हो?

usageContext: Unlike English 'for', 'seit' is used with the present tense in German if the action is still ongoing.
grammarNotes: Always requires the dative case.

description: An adjective or adverb describing a direction that is at a right angle to the horizon.

exampleSentences.english: The line runs vertically.
exampleSentences.hindi: रेखा लंबवत चलती है।

exampleSentences.german: Halten Sie die Flasche bitte senkrecht.
exampleSentences.english: Please hold the bottle upright.
exampleSentences.hindi: कृपया बोतल को सीधा खड़ा रखें।

usageContext: Used in technical contexts, giving directions for placement, or solving crosswords.
grammarNotes: As an adverb, it does not change; as an adjective, it takes standard endings.

description: Personal pronoun for third-person singular feminine (she) or third-person plural (they).

exampleSentences.english: She is my sister.
exampleSentences.hindi: वह मेरी बहन है।

exampleSentences.english: They live in Munich.
exampleSentences.hindi: वे म्यूनिख में रहते हैं।

usageContext: Must be distinguished from 'Sie' (formal you) by the context and verb conjugation.
grammarNotes: For 'she', the verb ends in '-t'. For 'they', the verb ends in '-en'. Written in lowercase unless at start of sentence.

description: The formal second-person pronoun used for both singular and plural 'you'.

exampleSentences.english: What is your name? (formal)
exampleSentences.hindi: आपका नाम क्या है?

exampleSentences.english: Can you help me?
exampleSentences.hindi: क्या आप मेरी मदद कर सकते हैं?

usageContext: Used with strangers, superiors, or in professional settings to show respect.
grammarNotes: Always capitalized in German. Verbs always end in '-en' (same as plural 'they').

description: The cardinal number following six and preceding eight.

exampleSentences.german: In einer Woche gibt es sieben Tage.
exampleSentences.english: There are seven days in a week.
exampleSentences.hindi: एक सप्ताह में सात दिन होते हैं।

exampleSentences.german: Wir treffen uns um sieben Uhr.
exampleSentences.english: We are meeting at seven o'clock.
exampleSentences.hindi: हम सात बजे मिल रहे हैं।

usageContext: Used for counting, time-telling, and expressing quantities.
grammarNotes: The initial 's' is voiced like a 'z'.

description: Used to agree with a statement or confirm that something is correct.

exampleSentences.german: Das ist dein Auto, oder? - Ja, stimmt.
exampleSentences.english: That's your car, right? - Yes, correct.
exampleSentences.hindi: यह तुम्हारी कार है, न? - हाँ, सही है।

exampleSentences.german: Das Wetter ist heute toll! - Stimmt!
exampleSentences.english: The weather is great today! - True!
exampleSentences.hindi: आज मौसम बहुत बढ़िया है! - बिल्कुल सही!

usageContext: Commonly used as a quick response to agree with someone or confirm a fact.
grammarNotes: Third-person singular form of the verb 'stimmen'.

description: The cardinal number 1,000.

exampleSentences.german: Das Dorf hat etwa tausend Einwohner.
exampleSentences.english: The village has about a thousand inhabitants.
exampleSentences.hindi: गाँव में लगभग एक हज़ार निवासी हैं।

exampleSentences.english: Thank you very much! - A thousand thanks!
exampleSentences.hindi: बहुत बहुत धन्यवाद! - हज़ारों धन्यवाद!

usageContext: Used for large quantities, prices, and as an intensifier in expressions like 'Tausend Dank'.
grammarNotes: Can be used as 'tausend' or 'eintausend' interchangeably when referring to the number 1,000.

description: A preposition indicating that something happened even though there was a reason for it not to.

exampleSentences.german: Trotz des Regens gehen wir spazieren.
exampleSentences.english: Despite the rain, we are going for a walk.
exampleSentences.hindi: बारिश के बावजूद हम सैर पर जा रहे हैं।

exampleSentences.english: He laughs despite his problems.
exampleSentences.hindi: अपनी समस्याओं के बावजूद वह हँसता है।

usageContext: Used to connect a circumstance with an unexpected result.
grammarNotes: Standardly requires the genitive case, though dative is common in spoken, informal German.

description: The most common informal way to say goodbye in Germany.

exampleSentences.english: I'm leaving now. Bye!
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं अब जा रहा हूँ। अलविदा!

exampleSentences.english: Bye, see you tomorrow!
exampleSentences.hindi: बाय, कल मिलते हैं!

usageContext: Used with friends, family, and in most casual daily encounters (shops, cafes).
grammarNotes: Can also be spelled 'tschüs'. It originated from the Low German 'adjüs'.

description: Used specifically for exact clock times and physical movement around an object.

exampleSentences.german: Der Film beginnt um acht Uhr.
exampleSentences.english: The movie starts at eight o'clock.
exampleSentences.hindi: फिल्म आठ बजे शुरू होती है।

exampleSentences.english: We are walking around the lake.
exampleSentences.hindi: हम झील के चारों ओर घूम रहे हैं।

usageContext: Essential for making appointments and describing physical position relative to a center point.
grammarNotes: Always requires the accusative case.

description: The basic coordinating conjunction used to join words, phrases, or clauses.

exampleSentences.german: Ich hätte gerne Brot und Butter.
exampleSentences.english: I would like bread and butter.
exampleSentences.hindi: मुझे ब्रेड और मक्खन चाहिए।

exampleSentences.german: Er spielt Fußball und sie spielt Tennis.
exampleSentences.english: He plays football and she plays tennis.
exampleSentences.hindi: वह फुटबॉल खेलता है और वह टेनिस खेलती है।

usageContext: Used in almost every conversation to list items or connect thoughts.
grammarNotes: Occupies 'position 0'; it does not affect the word order of the following clause.

description: The possessive pronoun for 'wir' (we).

exampleSentences.english: That is our house.
exampleSentences.hindi: यह हमारा घर है।

exampleSentences.english: Where is our teacher?
exampleSentences.hindi: हमारी शिक्षिका कहाँ हैं?

usageContext: Used to denote shared ownership or relationship.
grammarNotes: Declines according to the gender, case, and number of the noun it precedes (unser, unsere, unserem, etc.).

description: A two-way preposition indicating a position beneath something or being part of a group.

exampleSentences.german: Die Katze schläft unter dem Bett.
exampleSentences.english: The cat is sleeping under the bed.
exampleSentences.hindi: बिल्ली बिस्तर के नीचे सो रही है।

exampleSentences.english: He is among the guests.
exampleSentences.hindi: वह मेहमानों के बीच है।

usageContext: Used for physical locations and social groupings.
grammarNotes: A 'Wechselpräposition': takes dative for location (where?) and accusative for movement (where to?).

description: Used to describe a large quantity of countable items.

exampleSentences.english: I have many friends.
exampleSentences.hindi: मेरे बहुत सारे दोस्त हैं।

exampleSentences.german: Es gibt viele Autos in der Stadt.
exampleSentences.english: There are many cars in the city.
exampleSentences.hindi: शहर में बहुत सारी कारें हैं।

usageContext: Commonly used for people, objects, and any countable plural nouns.
grammarNotes: It is the plural form of 'viel'. Use 'viele' for countable things and 'viel' for uncountable things (like water/time).

description: The cardinal number following three and preceding five.

exampleSentences.english: We have four cats.
exampleSentences.hindi: हमारे पास चार बिल्लियाँ हैं।

exampleSentences.english: It is four o'clock in the afternoon.
exampleSentences.hindi: दोपहर के चार बजे हैं।

usageContext: Used for counting, expressing age, time, and quantities.
grammarNotes: The 'v' is pronounced like an 'f' in English.

description: A preposition indicating origin, possession (replacing the genitive), or the agent in passive sentences.

exampleSentences.german: Das ist das Auto von meinem Vater.
exampleSentences.english: That is my father's car (the car of my father).
exampleSentences.hindi: यह मेरे पिता की कार है।

exampleSentences.german: Ich komme gerade von der Arbeit.
exampleSentences.english: I am just coming from work.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं अभी काम से आ रहा हूँ।

usageContext: Used to show where someone/something is from or to show belonging in casual speech.
grammarNotes: Always requires the dative case. Often contracts with 'dem' to form 'vom'.

description: A two-way preposition indicating physical position (in front of) or time (before/ago).

exampleSentences.english: I am waiting in front of the cinema.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं सिनेमा हॉल के सामने इंतज़ार कर रहा हूँ।

exampleSentences.german: Vor zwei Jahren war ich in Indien.
exampleSentences.english: Two years ago I was in India.
exampleSentences.hindi: दो साल पहले मैं भारत में था।

usageContext: Crucial for describing spatial relationships and past timeframes ('ago').
grammarNotes: As a 'Wechselpräposition', it takes dative for location and accusative for movement. For time, it always takes the dative.

description: Describes a line or surface parallel to the horizon.

exampleSentences.english: Draw a horizontal line.
exampleSentences.hindi: एक क्षैतिज रेखा खींचें।

exampleSentences.english: The table must stand level/horizontal.
exampleSentences.hindi: मेज को बिल्कुल सीधा (आड़ा) खड़ा होना चाहिए।

usageContext: Used in technical contexts, architecture, or when giving specific spatial instructions.
grammarNotes: Often used in contrast with 'senkrecht'.

description: An interrogative pronoun used to ask about things, or an informal replacement for 'etwas'.

exampleSentences.english: What are you doing today?
exampleSentences.hindi: तुम आज क्या कर रहे हो?

exampleSentences.english: I have something for you.
exampleSentences.hindi: मेरे पास तुम्हारे लिए कुछ है।

usageContext: Standard word for asking questions about objects, actions, or situations.
grammarNotes: As a question word, it usually starts the sentence and triggers the verb in second position.

description: A preposition used to indicate the reason or cause for something.

exampleSentences.german: Wegen des Regens bleiben wir zu Hause.
exampleSentences.english: Because of the rain, we are staying at home.
exampleSentences.hindi: बारिश की वजह से हम घर पर रह रहे हैं।

exampleSentences.english: I am here because of you.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं तुम्हारी वजह से यहाँ हूँ।

usageContext: Used to explain the cause of a situation. Common in both daily speech and formal writing.
grammarNotes: Grammatically requires the genitive case (e.g., wegen des Regens), but in informal spoken German, the dative is very common (e.g., wegen dem Regen).

description: A subordinating conjunction used to introduce a clause that gives a reason.

exampleSentences.german: Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland arbeiten möchte.
exampleSentences.english: I am learning German because I want to work in Germany.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं जर्मन सीख रहा हूँ क्योंकि मैं जर्मनी में काम करना चाहता हूँ।

exampleSentences.german: Er ist glücklich, weil er Geburtstag hat.
exampleSentences.english: He is happy because it is his birthday.
exampleSentences.hindi: वह खुश है क्योंकि उसका जन्मदिन है।

usageContext: Essential for answering 'Warum' (Why) questions.
grammarNotes: It is a subordinating conjunction, which means it sends the conjugated verb to the very end of the clause.

description: An interrogative pronoun used to ask for a choice among a specific group.

exampleSentences.german: Welche Farbe gefällt dir am besten?
exampleSentences.english: Which color do you like best?
exampleSentences.hindi: तुम्हें कौन सा रंग सबसे ज्यादा पसंद है?

exampleSentences.english: Which book are you reading right now?
exampleSentences.hindi: तुम अभी कौन सी किताब पढ़ रहे हो?

usageContext: Used when picking from a defined set of options.
grammarNotes: The ending changes based on the gender, case, and number of the noun (welcher, welche, welches, welchen).

description: Used to describe a small number of countable things or people.

exampleSentences.german: Nur wenige Leute kamen zur Party.
exampleSentences.english: Only a few people came to the party.
exampleSentences.hindi: पार्टी में केवल कुछ ही लोग आए।

exampleSentences.german: Ich habe nur wenige Minuten Zeit.
exampleSentences.english: I only have a few minutes of time.
exampleSentences.hindi: मेरे पास केवल कुछ ही मिनट का समय है।

usageContext: Used for countable plural nouns to emphasize a small quantity.
grammarNotes: It is the plural form of 'wenig'. Use 'wenige' for countable items and 'wenig' for uncountable quantities (e.g., wenig Wasser).

description: A subordinating conjunction used for conditions (if) or repeated/future events (when).

exampleSentences.german: Wenn es regnet, bleibe ich zu Hause.
exampleSentences.english: If it rains, I stay at home.
exampleSentences.hindi: अगर बारिश होती है, तो मैं घर पर रहता हूँ।

exampleSentences.german: Sag mir Bescheid, wenn du bereit bist.
exampleSentences.english: Let me know when you are ready.
exampleSentences.hindi: जब तुम तैयार हो जाओ तो मुझे बता देना।

usageContext: Used for conditional 'if' and for 'when' in the present or future, or repeated past events.
grammarNotes: As a subordinating conjunction, it sends the verb to the end of the clause.

description: An interrogative pronoun used to ask about the identity of a person.

exampleSentences.english: Who is the man there?
exampleSentences.hindi: वहाँ वह आदमी कौन है?

exampleSentences.english: Who opened the window?
exampleSentences.hindi: खिड़की किसने खोली?

usageContext: Used as the subject of a sentence to identify people.
grammarNotes: It declines for case: wer (nom), wen (acc), wem (dat), wessen (gen).

description: First-person plural personal pronoun.

exampleSentences.english: We are going to the cinema today.
exampleSentences.hindi: हम आज सिनेमा जा रहे हैं।

exampleSentences.english: We are happy here.
exampleSentences.hindi: हम यहाँ खुश हैं।

usageContext: Used when the speaker is part of a group.
grammarNotes: In the nominative case. Changes to 'uns' in both accusative and dative cases.

description: Used to describe two events happening at the same time, either as a preposition or a conjunction.

exampleSentences.german: Während des Urlaubs hat es viel geregnet.
exampleSentences.english: During the holiday, it rained a lot.
exampleSentences.hindi: छुट्टियों के दौरान बहुत बारिश हुई।

exampleSentences.german: Ich höre Musik, während ich koche.
exampleSentences.english: I listen to music while I cook.
exampleSentences.hindi: जब मैं खाना बनाता हूँ, तो मैं संगीत सुनता हूँ।

usageContext: Used for simultaneous actions or time periods.
grammarNotes: As a preposition, it requires the genitive case. As a conjunction, it sends the verb to the end.

description: The cardinal number 10.

exampleSentences.english: It is ten o'clock.
exampleSentences.hindi: दस बजे हैं।

exampleSentences.english: That costs ten euros.
exampleSentences.hindi: इसकी कीमत दस यूरो है।

usageContext: Standard counting and quantities.
grammarNotes: The 'z' is pronounced like 'ts' in English.

description: A very versatile word used as a preposition of direction, an intensifier for adjectives, or to mean 'closed'.

exampleSentences.english: I am going to my brother's house.
exampleSentences.hindi: मैं अपने भाई के पास जा रहा हूँ।

exampleSentences.english: The soup is too hot.
exampleSentences.hindi: सूप बहुत ज़्यादा गर्म है।

usageContext: Used for going to people/specific places, or indicating an excess (too much).
grammarNotes: As a preposition, it always requires the dative case. Often contracts to 'zum' (zu + dem) or 'zur' (zu + der).

description: The cardinal number representing two tens (20).

exampleSentences.english: The book costs twenty euros.
exampleSentences.hindi: किताब की कीमत बीस यूरो है।

exampleSentences.english: He is twenty years old.
exampleSentences.hindi: वह बीस साल का है।

usageContext: Used for age, prices, quantities, and time (e.g., 20 minutes).
grammarNotes: The 'ig' ending in German is often pronounced like a soft 'ch' (ich-laut) in many regions.

description: The cardinal number between one and three.

exampleSentences.english: I have two sisters.
exampleSentences.hindi: मेरी दो बहनें हैं।

exampleSentences.english: It is two o'clock.
exampleSentences.hindi: दो बजे हैं।

usageContext: Standard counting. Sometimes pronounced as 'zwo' on the phone to avoid confusion with 'drei'.
grammarNotes: The 'z' is a sharp 'ts' sound. Diphthong 'ei' is always 'ai'.

description: A two-way preposition indicating a position in the middle of two or more points/people.

exampleSentences.german: Der Tisch steht zwischen den Stühlen.
exampleSentences.english: The table stands between the chairs.
exampleSentences.hindi: मेज कुर्सियों के बीच में है।

exampleSentences.german: Zwischen acht und neun Uhr habe ich Zeit.
exampleSentences.english: I have time between eight and nine o'clock.
exampleSentences.hindi: मेरे पास आठ और नौ बजे के बीच समय है।

usageContext: Used for both spatial (physical gap) and temporal (time gap) relationships.
grammarNotes: A 'Wechselpräposition': takes dative for location (Wo?) and accusative for movement (Wohin?).

description: A versatile preposition used for physical location (above) or to indicate a topic (about).

exampleSentences.german: Das Bild hängt über dem Sofa.
exampleSentences.english: The picture hangs above the sofa.
exampleSentences.hindi: तस्वीर सोफे के ऊपर टंगी है।

exampleSentences.english: We are talking about the movie.
exampleSentences.hindi: हम फिल्म के बारे में बात कर रहे हैं।

usageContext: Used physically for things not touching the surface below, or figuratively to discuss a subject.
grammarNotes: A 'Wechselpräposition' for location. When used to mean 'about', it always takes the accusative case.
